name: Big Tech Censorship
description: Big Tech Censorship, in the American context, refers to the belief that large technology companies (such as Facebook, Google, Twitter, and YouTube) systematically suppress or censor certain viewpoints, primarily those considered conservative or right-leaning. Proponents argue that these companies, often perceived as having a liberal bias, manipulate algorithms, deplatform users, and selectively enforce content moderation policies to silence dissenting voices and control the flow of information. Claims often center on the removal of accounts, posts, or videos belonging to prominent conservative figures, shadowbanning (reducing the visibility of content without outright removal), and the application of fact-checking labels deemed biased. The origins of this belief can be traced to growing concerns about the power and influence of tech companies in shaping public discourse, particularly following the 2016 US Presidential election and subsequent debates over misinformation and election interference. Mainstream scholarly perspectives acknowledge that content moderation policies exist and can disproportionately impact certain groups, but argue that these actions are often taken to combat hate speech, misinformation, and incitement to violence, and are not necessarily evidence of systematic political bias. Critics also point out that private companies have the right to set their own terms of service and moderate content accordingly, while emphasizing the need for transparency and accountability in content moderation practices. Furthermore, some argue that focusing solely on conservative censorship ignores the suppression of left-leaning voices and perspectives on these platforms, or the algorithmic amplification of extreme content from across the political spectrum.
subtitle: Alleged suppression of viewpoints online.

claims.claim: Big Tech companies systematically censor conservative viewpoints.
claims.evidence: Removal of conservative accounts and posts, shadowbanning, biased fact-checking.
claims.criticism: Content moderation policies are applied equally, targeting hate speech and misinformation, not specific political ideologies. Private companies have the right to set terms of service.

claims.claim: Algorithms are manipulated to suppress conservative content and boost liberal content.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal evidence of reduced reach, alleged algorithm bias.
claims.criticism: Algorithms are complex and prioritize user engagement, which can unintentionally affect content visibility. Algorithmic bias can exist but is not necessarily intentional or politically motivated.

claims.claim: Fact-checking is biased against conservative perspectives.
claims.evidence: Fact-checks disproportionately targeting conservative claims, perceived bias in fact-checkers' affiliations.
claims.criticism: Fact-checking aims to correct demonstrably false information, regardless of political affiliation. Fact-checkers adhere to professional standards and strive for impartiality.

keyFigures.name: Alex Jones
keyFigures.role: Prominent figure who was deplatformed from many platforms.

keyFigures.name: Mark Zuckerberg
keyFigures.role: CEO of Meta (Facebook), a frequent target of censorship claims.

keyFigures.name: Joan Donovan
keyFigures.role: Research Director of the Shorenstein Center on Media, Politics and Public Policy at Harvard Kennedy School, critiques misinformation and platform responsibility.

mythologyConnections: The concept of silencing dissenting voices echoes themes of oppression found in various mythologies.
The idea of a powerful, unseen force controlling information aligns with some interpretations of conspiratorial forces in mythology.

timeline.event: US Presidential Election fuels debates over misinformation and social media influence.
Alex Jones is deplatformed from major platforms for spreading conspiracy theories.
Debates over censorship intensify during the COVID-19 pandemic and the 2020 US Presidential Election.
Donald Trump is banned from Twitter and Facebook following the January 6th Capitol attack.

sources.author: Emily Van Der Nagel
sources.work: Platform Propaganda: How Facebook Became a Tool for Political Polarization
sources.title: Emily Van Der Nagel

sources.author: Sharyl Attkisson
sources.work: Slanted: How the News Media Taught America to Love Censorship and Hate Journalism
sources.title: Sharyl Attkisson

worshipPractices.name: The Algorithm Cleansing Ritual
worshipPractices.description: Followers meticulously review trending topics and flag content deemed heretical to the 'unbiased' narrative. This involves reporting, downvoting, and participating in online campaigns to suppress dissenting voices, aiming to purify the digital sphere.

worshipPractices.name: The Echo Chamber Confirmation
worshipPractices.description: Dedicated believers actively seek out and engage with content that reinforces their established viewpoints. Through selective consumption and the creation of online communities, they reaffirm their faith in the prevailing narrative and shield themselves from challenging perspectives.

festivals.name: The Day of Deplatforming
festivals.timing: Occurs whenever a prominent figure is banned or suspended from a major platform
festivals.description: Celebrated with online discussions, news articles, and social media posts analyzing the event and reaffirming the righteousness of the action. The event serves as a reminder of the power of the 'guardians' to maintain order.

festivals.name: The Integrity Update
festivals.timing: Whenever a major platform announces changes to its content moderation policies
festivals.description: Observed with intense scrutiny and debate, as followers analyze the new rules to determine if they further restrict 'harmful' speech or inadvertently allow 'misinformation' to spread. The event is a test of faith in the platform's commitment to truth.

cultural.modernLegacy: The concept is mirrored in contemporary debates about online freedom of speech and the responsibilities of social media platforms. Discussions about censorship and bias echo the mythical struggle between truth and falsehood, influencing policy debates and shaping public opinion about the digital landscape.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Big Tech platforms, like Pandora's Box, initially promised benefits (connectivity, information) but have unleashed unforeseen consequences, including the control and suppression of certain voices, leading to societal ills.

Mimir's Well represents a source of ultimate knowledge, but Odin had to sacrifice an eye to access it. Similarly, accessing information and participating in online discourse requires navigating algorithms and policies that can subtly control what is seen and heard, effectively sacrificing a degree of autonomy.

Ma'at represents cosmic order and truth. Big Tech platforms often claim to be upholding truth and combating misinformation, but critics argue that their definition of truth is subjective and used to silence dissenting opinions, disrupting the balance (Ma'at).

Maya refers to the illusion that veils the true nature of reality. Big Tech algorithms and curated content can create a distorted perception of reality, selectively filtering information and shaping narratives, thus contributing to a form of modern Maya.

associations.name: The Styx
associations.significance: Like the river Styx separating the living from the underworld, Big Tech censorship can create a barrier, preventing certain voices from reaching the mainstream or participating in public discourse.

associations.name: Argus
associations.significance: Argus, with his hundred eyes, represents the constant surveillance and monitoring employed by Big Tech platforms, enabling them to identify and potentially censor dissenting opinions.

associations.name: The Veil of Maya
associations.significance: Big Tech's algorithms can create a 'Veil of Maya,' obscuring the true nature of information and shaping user perception through selective filtering and promotion of content.

associations.name: Saturn
associations.significance: Saturn, often associated with restriction and limitation, symbolizes the way Big Tech censorship can constrict the free flow of information and limit individual expression.

associations.name: Quicksand
associations.significance: Big Tech censorship can act as quicksand, subtly trapping and silencing voices, making it difficult for marginalized perspectives to gain traction.

associations.name: Mistletoe
associations.significance: Mistletoe, in some mythologies, is seen as parasitic, drawing life from the host tree; similarly, Big Tech can be seen as parasitic, using user-generated content while potentially stifling alternative voices.

associations.name: Gray
associations.significance: Gray represents the ambiguity and moral complexities of Big Tech censorship, where the line between content moderation and viewpoint suppression is often blurred.

companions.name: Argus Panoptes
companions.description: Argus, the all-seeing giant, could serve as the ever-watchful algorithm, constantly scanning and filtering content based on pre-determined rules and biases. He represents the inescapable surveillance and judgment of the platform.

companions.name: Procrustes
companions.description: Procrustes, who forced travelers to fit his bed by stretching or cutting them, embodies the act of forcing diverse opinions into a narrow, acceptable mold. He symbolizes the standardization and homogenization of thought under censorship.

companions.name: The Sirens
companions.description: The Sirens lull users into echo chambers with tailored content, making them less likely to seek out or engage with opposing viewpoints. This carefully curated content creates a deceptive sense of agreement and reinforces existing beliefs.

companions.name: Momus
companions.description: Momus, the Greek god of mockery and criticism, now acts as the instigator, always finding fault and justification for silencing dissenting voices under the guise of protecting others. He represents the weaponization of moral outrage to justify censorship.

companions.name: The Hydra
companions.description: Each time a piece of misinformation is censored, several others appear in its place, making it very difficult to eradicate, like the multiple heads of the hydra. Censorship ends up being an unending battle.

extendedContent.title: The Muzzled Oracle: Suppressing Prophecy and Dissent
extendedContent.content: Across numerous mythologies, oracles and prophets served as vital conduits to divine knowledge and societal guidance. However, their pronouncements were not always welcome, particularly when they challenged the status quo or foretold unfavorable events. The silencing or manipulation of oracles represents a mythological parallel to concerns about modern censorship. In Greek mythology, Cassandra, cursed by Apollo to foresee the future but never be believed, embodies the frustration of having truth dismissed. Her warnings about the Trojan Horse went unheeded, leading to Troy's downfall, a potent metaphor for the consequences of ignoring dissenting voices. The deliberate misinterpretation or suppression of oracular pronouncements, as seen in the manipulation of prophecies related to Oedipus, further underscores the dangers of controlling information. In Norse mythology, the Völva, a seeress capable of glimpsing Ragnarök, faced societal pressures and potential persecution for her visions of destruction, highlighting the tension between prophecy and power. These mythological instances illustrate how the control of information, even divinely sourced, has historically been used to maintain power structures and suppress unwanted narratives. The act of silencing or manipulating oracles serves as a cautionary tale about the potential consequences of limiting access to diverse perspectives and independent thought.

extendedContent.title: The Library of Alexandria's Echo: Loss of Knowledge, Loss of Power
extendedContent.content: The burning of the Library of Alexandria, though a historical event, carries significant mythological weight, symbolizing the catastrophic loss of knowledge and the suppression of intellectual discourse. While the exact circumstances surrounding its destruction remain debated, the event has become a potent symbol of the fragility of information and the devastating consequences of its destruction. In many cultures, libraries and archives are seen as repositories of collective memory and wisdom, representing the accumulated knowledge of generations. The destruction of such resources, whether intentional or accidental, signifies a deliberate or negligent erasure of history and a crippling of intellectual progress. The Library of Alexandria's fate resonates with concerns about the potential for digital censorship to lead to a similar loss of access to information. While digital content may not be physically destroyed in the same way as ancient scrolls, the removal of websites, the deplatforming of users, and the manipulation of search results can effectively render information inaccessible, creating a modern-day digital equivalent of the Library's demise. The fear is that curated narratives and controlled access to information, similar to the loss of Alexandria's vast collection, limit intellectual exploration and hinder the pursuit of truth.

extendedContent.title: Pandora's Box and the Algorithmic Dilemma: Unintended Consequences of Control
extendedContent.content: Pandora's Box, a central myth in Greek lore, illustrates the dangers of attempting to control information or contain potentially harmful content. Pandora, instructed not to open the box (or jar), ultimately succumbs to curiosity, releasing a multitude of evils upon the world. While the myth is often interpreted as a cautionary tale against disobedience, it also highlights the unintended consequences that can arise from attempts to suppress or control knowledge. In the context of modern censorship, the algorithmic curation of content on social media platforms can be seen as a contemporary Pandora's Box. While algorithms are designed to filter out harmful or inappropriate content, they can also inadvertently create echo chambers, reinforce biases, and suppress dissenting voices. The act of attempting to control the flow of information through complex algorithms can have unforeseen and potentially detrimental effects, similar to the unleashing of evils from Pandora's Box. The challenge lies in balancing the need to protect users from harmful content with the imperative to preserve freedom of expression and access to diverse perspectives. The Pandora's Box myth serves as a reminder that attempts to control information, however well-intentioned, can have unintended and far-reaching consequences.

extendedContent.title: The Tower of Babel and the Fragmentation of Discourse: The Peril of Uniformity
extendedContent.content: The Tower of Babel narrative, found in the Book of Genesis, tells the story of humanity's attempt to build a tower reaching to heaven, an act seen as challenging God's authority. As punishment, God confounds their language, scattering them across the earth and hindering their ability to communicate and collaborate. While primarily a story about hubris, the Tower of Babel myth also speaks to the importance of diverse perspectives and the dangers of enforced uniformity. The confusion of languages represents a fragmentation of discourse, a breakdown in communication that prevents collective action and shared understanding. In the context of modern censorship, the suppression of certain viewpoints can be seen as a form of linguistic or intellectual homogenization. When dissenting voices are silenced or marginalized, the range of perspectives available to the public is narrowed, leading to a less nuanced and informed understanding of complex issues. The Tower of Babel myth serves as a cautionary tale against the pursuit of uniformity and the suppression of diverse voices. A healthy society requires a robust marketplace of ideas, where different perspectives can be debated and challenged, fostering intellectual growth and informed decision-making. The attempt to impose a single narrative or suppress dissenting viewpoints, like the singular language of Babel, ultimately leads to fragmentation and misunderstanding.
